metric_name: Kastner joins Prime Computer
metric_value: Arrived at Prime Computer around Thanksgiving 1979, joining Market Planning group.

metric_name: Physical campus atmosphere at Prime Park
metric_value: Prime Park on Route 9 in Natick; new glass going up, rental cars crowding visitor spots, headhunters in better suits than engineers.

metric_name: Prime Computer revenue at Kastner arrival
metric_value: CEO Ken Fisher had taken Prime from $10 million to a serious growth franchise over six years.

metric_name: Ken Fisher tenure as Prime CEO
metric_value: Six years as CEO, growing Prime from $10M hopeful to serious growth franchise by time Kastner arrived.

metric_name: Prime Computer competitive positioning
metric_value: By 1980, Prime appeared in the same analyst slide decks as DEC, Data General, and HP as a potential challenger to IBM from below.

metric_name: Prime Computer stock performance
metric_value: In 1980, Prime was the number-one moving stock on the New York Stock Exchange, up approximately 272 percent for the year.

metric_name: Chuck Casale role at Prime and later Aberdeen
metric_value: Investor Relations VP at Prime; stock up 272% made his job easy; later became Kastner’s partner at Aberdeen Group.

metric_name: Kastner pre-Prime background contrast
metric_value: Eight years building systems under fixed-price contracts at ADL, absorbing all risk with capped upside; Prime represented visible equity upside.

metric_name: Prime go-to-market philosophy
metric_value: Hybrid of mainframe discipline and Route 128 swagger; named accounts, territorial sales, solution selling around business problems rather than raw MIPS.

metric_name: Solution-selling concealment of underlying platform
metric_value: Sold as 'online banking platform' or 'interactive engineering environment'; customer discovered later they bought a Prime 750 running PRIMOS and INFORMATION.

metric_name: Prime INFORMATION architecture description
metric_value: Based on Pick OS; used multi-value dynamic array fields; data organized the way business thinks it; no table joins needed.

metric_name: Pick architecture provenance
metric_value: Pick OS developed in the 1960s for inventory and distribution systems; used multi-value fields in dynamic arrays vs. relational column-row structure.

metric_name: INFORMATION strength for commercial applications
metric_value: Multi-value records mapped naturally to real-world data (variable transactions per account, variable line items per PO) without table joins.

metric_name: INFORMATION weakness: proprietary lock-in
metric_value: Every application built on Pick/INFORMATION was captive to Prime hardware; as open systems and relational DBs grew, lock-in became liability not feature.

metric_name: INFORMATION customer narrative in 1980
metric_value: Faster application development, tighter integration, no army of COBOL programmers required.

metric_name: Prime argument against DEC VAX
metric_value: Responsiveness: shorter application cycles, tighter integration; mid-sized regional bank could look like national institution on modest minicomputer footprint.

metric_name: Prime VAR/OEM ecosystem
metric_value: VARs, OEMs, and ISVs building vertical packages on INFORMATION served as force multipliers; 'PR1ME shops' were an exclusive club with own language and lore.

metric_name: Prime field office admin ratio
metric_value: One-to-one ratio of sales executives to sales administrators in field offices; far better than most companies; kept paperwork inside legal guardrails at quarter-end.

metric_name: Compliance contrast: Prime vs. CA and Informix
metric_value: Unlike Computer Associates and Informix (later Aberdeen clients), no one from Prime ever went to jail for cooking the books.

metric_name: Informix accounting fraud reference
metric_value: Named alongside Computer Associates as Aberdeen clients where executives faced legal consequences for revenue manipulation; contrasted with Prime’s compliance.

metric_name: Schlegel recruits Kastner
metric_value: Ran Market Planning at Prime; was the brother of an ADL colleague; recruited Kastner and gave him commercial vertical markets, alongside Steve Franson.

metric_name: Schlegel reassigned to Prime Office launch
metric_value: By Christmas 1979, Schlegel was given the job of launching Prime Office (office automation) and moved to product planning.

metric_name: Franson becomes EA to sales VP
metric_value: Became executive assistant to sales VP Bob Clausen and moved upstairs shortly after Kastner joined.

metric_name: Kastner left with all commercial customers nominally
metric_value: After Schlegel and Franson departed their roles, Kastner was nominally left with 'all commercial customers.'

metric_name: Territory shrinkage from technical reclassification
metric_value: CAD/CAM at Ford and avionics at General Dynamics reclassified as technical/scientific; Kastner’s commercial client roster shrank accordingly.

metric_name: Ford as Prime CAD/CAM customer
metric_value: Ford’s CAD/CAM work at Prime classified as technical/scientific, not commercial; removed from Kastner’s territory.

metric_name: General Dynamics as Prime avionics customer
metric_value: Avionics work at General Dynamics classified as technical/scientific; removed from Kastner’s commercial territory.

metric_name: Lake Tahoe sales kickoff culture
metric_value: Bob Clausen at craps table, making thousand-dollar bets, with thirty salespeople cheering; followed by product roadmaps, skiing at Heavenly, awards banquet.

metric_name: Lake Tahoe kickoff as cultural indicator
metric_value: High confidence, high energy, absolute certainty good times would keep rolling; machine printing money, stock up 272%; no appetite for downside scenarios.

metric_name: Kastner reaction to Prime sales culture
metric_value: Coming from eight years at ADL pricing risk on fixed-fee contracts, the culture was exhilarating and slightly alarming in equal measure.

metric_name: Tarulli wins director role over Kastner
metric_value: Dick Tarulli, district sales manager from Ohio, beat Kastner for the director’s job; reorganized territory along more conventional vertical lines.

metric_name: Kastner assigned government vertical market
metric_value: Government ended up with Kastner after territorial reorganization; turned out to be one of the more interesting assignments of his career.

metric_name: International government market trips
metric_value: Government vertical required trips to England, France, Japan, and Australia to understand global markets.

metric_name: GCHQ Prime system for satellite photo analysis
metric_value: Kastner visited GCHQ in Cheltenham; a Prime system was doing satellite photo analysis on the Iran-Iraq war.

metric_name: CIA and MI6 security vetting for GCHQ visit
metric_value: Kastner vetted by CIA and MI6 prior to GCHQ visit; facility appeared as normal people in normal offices doing anything-but-normal work.

metric_name: MI6 involvement in GCHQ vetting
metric_value: Co-vetted Kastner with CIA for GCHQ site access in Cheltenham.

metric_name: French government market characteristics
metric_value: In France, government was roughly half the entire economy; every regional Prime opportunity traced back to Paris.

metric_name: Japanese government market landscape
metric_value: Local Prime manager in Japan played tour guide through a government landscape dense with ministries and procurement rituals.

metric_name: Australian dealer TV advertising with Doctor Who
metric_value: Lionel Singer (Australia) placed Prime Computer in TV commercials alongside the popular Doctor Who TV show; it worked; unprecedented in minicomputer industry.

metric_name: Minicomputer TV advertising breakthrough
metric_value: Lionel Singer’s Australian Doctor Who co-promotion was something no one else in the minicomputer industry had managed; successfully put Prime on television.

metric_name: Reagan defense buildup as tailwind
metric_value: Reagan defense buildup was creating a steady tailwind for Prime’s government business; Kastner got a New York Times quote during this period.
source_page: PROMIS and the New Jersey Caper

metric_name: PROMIS as Prime’s first notable government initiative
metric_value: PROMIS prosecutor case-management system, developed by INSLAW under federal LEAA grants, caught Prime’s government team attention.
source_page: PROMIS and the New Jersey Caper

metric_name: PROMIS system capabilities
metric_value: Tracked defendants, charges, hearings, evidence, and outcomes in unified database; gave prosecutors complete picture of every case in real time.

metric_name: DOJ PROMIS deployment contract with Prime hardware
metric_value: DOJ awarded INSLAW contract to deploy PROMIS across federal prosecutor system; Prime won hardware business for more than twenty systems.

metric_name: PROMIS later legal controversy
metric_value: INSLAW later alleged DOJ stole its software and sold it to foreign intelligence agencies; sparked years of congressional investigations and litigation.

metric_name: PROMIS as early applications generator example
metric_value: At the time of selling hardware, PROMIS was simply an impressive early example of an applications-generator for information management in public safety.

metric_name: PROMIS connects to Kastner’s ADL law enforcement work
metric_value: INSLAW used the same LEAA grant stream that funded ADL’s law enforcement systems work with police departments in Florida and Virginia years earlier.

metric_name: PROMIS state-level push program
metric_value: Prime field team launched Kastner’s program to push PROMIS at the state level as well as federal.
source_page: PROMIS and the New Jersey Caper

metric_name: New Jersey bid non-compliance incident
metric_value: Local team delivered only 4 of 7 required proposal copies by deadline; returned after dark, found unlocked window, delivered missing copies to bid room in Trenton.
source_page: PROMIS and the New Jersey Caper

metric_name: Corporate counsel explains bid non-compliance limits
metric_value: Corporate counsel Bernie Bradstreet patiently explained why Prime would not sue New Jersey over a clearly non-compliant bid.
source_page: PROMIS and the New Jersey Caper

metric_name: Prime cultural character summary
metric_value: Enormous initiative, occasionally creative interpretations of process, and just enough adult supervision to keep everyone out of jail.
source_page: PROMIS and the New Jersey Caper

metric_name: Prime Office delivered to White House
metric_value: Schlegel delivered Prime Office running on a Model 750 to the White House as the first office automation system at federal executive level.

metric_name: White House as Prime Office reference account
metric_value: The White House was the first office automation system at federal executive level; described as a reference account that stopped conversations cold in any sales meeting.

metric_name: Kastner leads vertical market product requirements project
metric_value: In 1981, Tarulli assigned Kastner to lead project defining future product requirements for all of Prime’s planned vertical markets; months of customer/partner/prospect interviews.

metric_name: Prime product requirements project as Aberdeen rehearsal
metric_value: Project was complete rehearsal for Aberdeen Group decade: segment market, identify customer’s actual problem, quantify gap, map competitive landscape.

metric_name: Prime document reads like early Aberdeen report
metric_value: The 1981 product requirements document still exists in Kastner’s archive; reads like an early Aberdeen report: structured, market-focused, slightly over-optimistic on adoption pace.

metric_name: Ken Fisher resignation from Prime
metric_value: From inside Prime Park, Fisher’s 'resignation' in 1981 felt like the board blinking first in a long argument about direction and pace, not a midlife sabbatical.
source_page: The Fisher Departure and the Exit Signal

metric_name: Fisher’s vision for Prime’s future
metric_value: Fisher’s instinct in 1981: keep pressing — bigger architectural bets, more ambitious moves beyond midrange, explicit push to make Prime a billion-dollar peer of DEC.
source_page: The Fisher Departure and the Exit Signal

metric_name: Board concerns about Fisher’s growth strategy
metric_value: Board worried about overreach: capital intensity of Fisher’s roadmap, cultural strain of permanent rocket ship, engineering team stretched beyond capacity.
source_page: The Fisher Departure and the Exit Signal

metric_name: Expected post-Fisher successor profile
metric_value: Next chapter expected to be consolidation under imported IBM executive who would 'professionalize' go-to-market machine; first act: reorganize staff marketing jobs.
source_page: The Fisher Departure and the Exit Signal

metric_name: Kastner updates resume after Fisher departure
metric_value: After reading the post-Fisher signal as a consolidation leading to staff marketing reorganization, Kastner updated his resume.
source_page: The Fisher Departure and the Exit Signal

metric_name: Stratus Computer calls Kastner
metric_value: Not long after updating his resume, a startup called Stratus Computer called Kastner; he departed Prime for Stratus.
source_page: The Fisher Departure and the Exit Signal

metric_name: Best technology does not automatically win
metric_value: Prime INFORMATION was genuinely ahead of conventional COBOL-based systems for commercial applications in 1980, yet ultimately lost due to proprietary architecture.

metric_name: Proprietary lock-in as asset in good times, trap in bad
metric_value: Every INFORMATION application created customer lock-in; asset in good times, strategic prison when relational databases gathered force in mid-1980s.

metric_name: INFORMATION ecosystem had no migration path
metric_value: When relational database movement gathered force in mid-1980s, Prime’s non-relational, non-standard application ecosystem had nowhere to migrate.

metric_name: Prime’s 1980 narrative: true short-term, wrong long-term
metric_value: Tightly integrated hw/OS/application delivering faster results than IBM-compatible systems: true and compelling in 1980, wrong in the long run as x86/Unix economics took over.
source_page: A Note on Timing and Narrative

metric_name: Minicomputer vendors all telling same correct-then-wrong story
metric_value: DEC, Data General, and a dozen other minicomputer vendors were all telling same story in different dialects; all correct short run, almost all wrong long run.
source_page: A Note on Timing and Narrative

metric_name: Minicomputer era ended by x86/open OS economics
metric_value: Minicomputer era ended not because hardware got worse, but because economics of standardized x86 and open OSes overwhelmed performance/integration advantages of proprietary platforms.
source_page: A Note on Timing and Narrative

metric_name: Companies reading the shift early survived
metric_value: Companies that read the x86/open OS shift early survived; Prime’s leadership was not among them; the others became acquisition targets or museum pieces.
source_page: A Note on Timing and Narrative

metric_name: Correct short-term story, wrong long-term architecture as recurring pattern
metric_value: This pattern — correct short-term story, wrong long-term architecture — recurred throughout Kastner’s next two decades at Aberdeen; recognizing it became a core analytical skill.
source_page: A Note on Timing and Narrative

metric_name: Sales culture as leading indicator of disruption risk
metric_value: Prime salesforce talented, aggressive, genuinely believed in product; but Lake Tahoe craps table culture was a leading indicator: organizations that cannot imagine disruption get disrupted.

metric_name: Prime could not imagine Intel/Unix disruption
metric_value: Prime in 1980 could not imagine the world where an Intel-based server running Unix would render their minicomputer architecture economically irrelevant.

metric_name: Prime teaches the defining analytical question
metric_value: Left for Stratus with sharper sense of the defining question: not 'who is winning today?' but 'what happens to today’s winner when the economics change?'

metric_name: Prime 750 as flagship model for solutions
metric_value: Customer discovered they had bought a Prime 750 running PRIMOS, Prime networking, and a 4GL database environment descended from Pick architecture.

metric_name: Prime INFORMATION target verticals
metric_value: Banking, insurance, service bureaus were named as strong commercial application fits for Prime INFORMATION’s multi-value record structure.

metric_name: Prime Park headquarters location
metric_value: Prime Computer headquartered at Prime Park on Route 9 in Natick, Massachusetts.

metric_name: Prime stock ticker name
metric_value: Prime Computer traded under ticker PR1ME; investor question was 'So, what’s your PR1ME position?'

metric_name: Prime territory structure: technical vs commercial
metric_value: Internal rule: customers doing anything technical or scientific (CAD/CAM, avionics) belonged to technical side, not commercial territory.

metric_name: Prime field office structure: named accounts, territorial offices
metric_value: Classic minicomputer terms: named accounts, territorial sales offices, heavy sales-engineering overlay, packaged around specific business problems.

metric_name: Prime government market scope
metric_value: Government vertical required international coverage: England (GCHQ), France, Japan, Australia, plus U.S. federal programs like PROMIS and Reagan defense buildup.

metric_name: Prime PROMIS hardware win: more than 20 systems
metric_value: Prime won hardware business for more than twenty PROMIS deployments across federal prosecutor system.

metric_name: Prime Office: White House first federal executive OA system
metric_value: Prime Office running on Model 750 delivered to White House as first office automation system at federal executive level.

metric_name: Kastner tenure at Prime Computer
metric_value: Approximately two years at Prime Computer, Thanksgiving 1979 to late 1981.

metric_name: Technology ecosystem as economics/vertical/narrative, not just technical competition
metric_value: Two years at Prime completed education ADL began: technology industry is ecosystem of economics, vertical markets, positioning, sales culture, and narrative.

metric_name: Prime INFORMATION lock-in became strategic prison by mid-1980s
metric_value: Prime’s application ecosystem, built on non-relational, non-standard Pick foundation, had nowhere to migrate when relational database movement gathered force.

metric_name: Intel/Unix server would render Prime architecture irrelevant
metric_value: Prime in 1980 could not imagine an Intel-based server running Unix making their minicomputer architecture economically irrelevant; Kastner saw it coming.

metric_name: Consolidation strategy post-Fisher: professionalize vs press
metric_value: Fisher wanted bigger bets and billion-dollar scale; board chose consolidation, likely under IBM executive to professionalize; these were incompatible visions.
source_page: The Fisher Departure and the Exit Signal

metric_name: Prime sales kickoff: Lake Tahoe, Heavenly ski resort
metric_value: Annual sales kickoff held at Lake Tahoe; included skiing at Heavenly; attended by approximately 30 salespeople plus leadership.

metric_name: Prime Boston Harbor Hotel investor engagement
metric_value: Portfolio managers at Boston Harbor Hotel asking about PR1ME position at lunch; indicator of Prime’s Wall Street profile.

metric_name: Kastner’s ADL law enforcement systems connection to PROMIS
metric_value: PROMIS connected to Kastner’s earlier ADL work with police departments in Florida and Virginia under LEAA grants; same federal funding stream.

metric_name: 1981 vertical market product requirements document
metric_value: Kastner synthesized months of customer/partner interviews into structured product requirements document; still exists in his archive.

metric_name: Aberdeen Group methodology roots at Prime
metric_value: Analytical discipline identical between Prime internal work and Aberdeen client work: segment, identify actual problem, quantify gap, map competitive landscape.

metric_name: Prime report slightly over-optimistic on adoption pace
metric_value: 1981 product requirements document slightly more optimistic about pace of technology adoption than events would warrant — a characteristic Kastner notes recurred at Aberdeen.

metric_name: Prime 50-series sales framing
metric_value: You didn’t sell a 750 or a 50-series; you sold 'an on-line banking platform' or 'an interactive engineering environment.'

metric_name: Pick multi-value architecture vs relational: 1980 advantage
metric_value: In 1980, multi-value records were genuinely better for many commercial apps than relational tables; customer account with variable transactions didn’t need joins.

metric_name: DEC VAX as Prime’s primary competitive benchmark
metric_value: Against DEC’s VAX ecosystem, Prime’s argument was responsiveness: shorter application cycles, tighter integration, modest minicomputer footprint for mid-sized organizations.

metric_name: PR1ME shops as exclusive user community
metric_value: 'PR1ME shops' were a club with their own language and lore; felt exclusive in a good way; VARs/OEMs/ISVs served as force multipliers for Prime.

metric_name: Market Planning group structure at Prime
metric_value: Peter Schlegel ran group; Kastner covered commercial vertical markets; Steve Franson worked alongside; lasted only as long as Thanksgiving leftovers before reorganization.

metric_name: Prime minicomputer market peer set
metric_value: DEC, Data General, and HP named as the peer set in analyst slide decks; all positioned as potential IBM challengers from below.

metric_name: Data General as minicomputer narrative peer of Prime
metric_value: Data General told same correct-short-run, wrong-long-run minicomputer story in its own dialect; ultimately also became an acquisition target.
source_page: A Note on Timing and Narrative

metric_name: DEC as minicomputer narrative peer of Prime
metric_value: DEC told same correct-short-run, wrong-long-run minicomputer story; DEC’s VAX ecosystem ultimately also lost to x86/Unix economics.
source_page: A Note on Timing and Narrative

metric_name: INFORMATION’s COBOL displacement pitch
metric_value: Prime INFORMATION positioned as eliminating need for army of COBOL programmers; faster application development and tighter integration.

metric_name: Non-relational vs relational: Prime’s architectural bet
metric_value: Prime INFORMATION’s non-relational, non-standard Pick foundation had nowhere to migrate when relational database movement gathered force in mid-1980s.

metric_name: x86 standardization ends minicomputer era
metric_value: Economics of standardized x86 architecture plus open operating systems would overwhelm the performance and integration advantages of all proprietary minicomputer platforms.
source_page: A Note on Timing and Narrative

metric_name: Prime Computer founded circa 1973, turning point 1979
metric_value: In six years (from roughly 1973), Ken Fisher took Prime from $10M to a Wall Street rocket ship status; Kastner arrived at the inflection point.
notes: Exact founding year inferred from '6 years' context; approximate.

metric_name: Annual sales kickoff agenda structure
metric_value: Night 1: craps; Day 2: product roadmaps; Day 3: skiing at Heavenly + awards banquet; Day 4: back on plane.

metric_name: Kastner on periphery of White House Prime Office project
metric_value: Kastner was on the periphery of the White House Prime Office project; Schlegel delivered it; described as a resume line that stopped conversations cold.

metric_name: Pick architecture for commercial data structures
metric_value: Customer account could hold variable number of transaction records; purchase order could hold variable line items; no table joins needed unlike relational models.

metric_name: Prime Model 750 used for White House Prime Office
metric_value: White House Prime Office system ran on a Model 750; first office automation system at federal executive level.

metric_name: PROMIS development timeline
metric_value: PROMIS developed by INSLAW in the 1970s under federal grant to automate management of criminal cases.

metric_name: Prime’s go-to-market: 'Wall Street rocket ship' era
metric_value: Prime was turning from scrappy minicomputer vendor into a fully-fledged Wall Street rocket ship by the time Kastner arrived Thanksgiving 1979.

metric_name: Ken Fisher departure year
metric_value: Ken Fisher resigned/was pushed out of Prime Computer in 1981 after board disagreement over growth pace and capital allocation.
source_page: The Fisher Departure and the Exit Signal

metric_name: Fisher departure signal: consolidation vs growth
metric_value: Fisher departure signaled board preferred consolidation to aggressive growth; post-Fisher direction was professionalization, not moonshots.
source_page: The Fisher Departure and the Exit Signal

metric_name: Prime billion-dollar aspiration under Fisher
metric_value: Fisher wanted Prime to become a billion-dollar peer of DEC rather than a tidy niche minicomputer player.
source_page: The Fisher Departure and the Exit Signal

metric_name: Board risk factors in rejecting Fisher roadmap
metric_value: Capital intensity of Fisher’s roadmap; cultural strain of permanent rocket-ship culture; engineering team stretched past what it could sustain.
source_page: The Fisher Departure and the Exit Signal

metric_name: Minicomputer industry ‘correct short-term story, wrong long-term architecture’ pattern
metric_value: All proprietary minicomputer vendors were correct in the short run and almost all were wrong in the long run; those who read the shift early survived.
source_page: A Note on Timing and Narrative

metric_name: Prime INFORMATION market narrative
metric_value: Clean narrative in 1980: faster application development, tighter integration, no army of COBOL programmers required; true at the time.

metric_name: Iran-Iraq war satellite photo analysis at GCHQ on Prime
metric_value: A Prime system at GCHQ in Cheltenham was doing satellite photo analysis on the Iran-Iraq war.

metric_name: GCHQ appearance: normal people, extraordinary work
metric_value: GCHQ visit was unremarkable on the surface — normal people in normal offices — doing work that, once understood, was anything but normal.

metric_name: French government market: centralization in Paris
metric_value: In France, government was roughly half the entire economy; every regional Prime opportunity traced back to Paris.

metric_name: Japanese government: dense ministries and procurement rituals
metric_value: Local Prime manager in Japan played tour guide through government landscape dense with ministries and procurement rituals.

metric_name: Doctor Who co-branding as minicomputer advertising innovation
metric_value: Lionel Singer placed Prime Computer in Australian TV commercials alongside Doctor Who — something no one else in the minicomputer industry had managed; it worked.

metric_name: New York Times quote during Reagan defense buildup
metric_value: Kastner received a New York Times quote during the Reagan defense buildup tailwind for Prime’s government business.
source_page: PROMIS and the New Jersey Caper

metric_name: Reagan defense buildup as government IT tailwind
metric_value: Reagan defense buildup created a steady tailwind for Prime’s government vertical in 1980-1981.
source_page: PROMIS and the New Jersey Caper

metric_value: See companion study kastner-technology-breadth-memoir-2026 for quantified 47-year coverage breadth (2,537 distinct technologies, 14 thematic rollups).
